marriages.notes: Stage 6Y-202: eldest surviving child TD0238 built (Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones, m. Amasa Jones 1794, → Wilkes-Barre PA 1816). 8 other M2 children deferred (Talcott gen 6 cohort, mostly died young): Septimus 1773-1776, George Washington Huntington 1776-1777, Septimus George 1778-1844, Hannah 1779-1794, Henry 1781-1806, Lucretia Norton 1783-1876, Mercy Penelope 1788-1794, James 1790-1793.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of typhus at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt Jonathan Hale Jr died of camp fever at Roxbury MA on March 7, 1776 — less than four years after her August 1772 marriage to Joseph Huntington. Her older brother Jonathan died unmarried that October and her younger sister Jerusha died that September, leaving her mother widowed with three children buried in one Revolutionary year.

narrative_blocks.header: Six of nine children predeceased her
narrative_blocks.body: Of the nine children she bore Rev Joseph Huntington between 1773 and 1790, six died before she did — Septimus (age 3), George Washington (age 1), Hannah (age 15), Penelope (age 6), James (age 3), and Henry (age 25 same year as her own death). Only Elizabeth, Septimus George, and Lucretia outlived their mother to maturity.

marriages.notes: Stage 6Y-202: eldest surviving child TD0238 built (Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones, m. Amasa Jones 1794, → Wilkes-Barre PA 1816). 8 other M2 children deferred (Talcott gen 6 cohort, mostly died young): Septimus 1773-1776, George Washington Huntington 1776-1777, Septimus George 1778-1844, Hannah 1779-1794, Henry 1781-1806, Lucretia Norton 1783-1876, Mercy Penelope 1788-1794, James 1790-1793.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died of typhus at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt Jonathan Hale Jr died of camp fever at Roxbury MA on March 7, 1776 — less than four years after her August 1772 marriage to Joseph Huntington. Her older brother Jonathan died unmarried that October and her younger sister Jerusha died that September, leaving her mother widowed with three children buried in one Revolutionary year.

narrative_blocks.header: Six of nine children predeceased her
narrative_blocks.body: Of the nine children she bore Rev Joseph Huntington between 1773 and 1790, six died before she did — Septimus (age 3), George Washington (age 1), Hannah (age 15), Penelope (age 6), James (age 3), and Henry (age 25 same year as her own death). Only Elizabeth, Septimus George, and Lucretia outlived their mother to maturity.

death.cause: Complication of diseases per contemporary account

education.notes: Yale College B.A. 1762. Entered at age 23 after his father had destined him for the clothier trade against his own wishes; his pastor Rev. Ebenezer Devotion encouraged the late preparation. Graduated honorably.

career.notes: Installed June 29, 1763 — within one year of his Yale graduation. Continued as pastor until his death thirty-one years later. Under his ministry the parish built the best meeting house in the region for $5,000 — services at his ordination were held in the open air either because the existing meeting house was too small or too dilapidated.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1762; D.D. honoris causa 1780; Trustee 1780-1788.

notable.notable_blurb: Coventry minister who posthumously published Universalism

cross_connections.display_label: Tutored Nathan Hale at Coventry for Yale entrance

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter burned most copies of his Universalist treatise
narrative_blocks.body: 'Calvinism Improved' — his posthumous 1796 treatise defending universal salvation and denying eternal punishment — was doomed to limited circulation because one of his daughters consigned the greater part of the edition to the flames. Sprague's Annals of the American Pulpit notes 'so effectual was the suppression of the work that it is now almost impossible to find a copy.'

narrative_blocks.header: Son from first marriage became Ohio Governor
narrative_blocks.body: His eldest son with Hannah Devotion — Samuel H. Huntington — was orphaned at age six when Hannah died in 1771 and was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington, the Connecticut Governor and Declaration signer. The boy grew up to attend Dartmouth and Yale, settled Cleveland in 1801, and became the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 — half-brother to the nine children Joseph would later have with second wife Elizabeth Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Tutored Nathan Hale of Coventry for Yale
narrative_blocks.body: He prepared Nathan Hale (1755-1776) of Coventry for Yale before Hale entered the college in 1769 and graduated 1773. Hale was hanged at New York September 22, 1776 — 'I only regret that I have but one life to lose for my country.' Both Huntington and Hale are buried at the Nathan Hale Cemetery in Coventry.

narrative_blocks.header: Almost succeeded Wheelock as Dartmouth president, 1780
narrative_blocks.body: After the death of Rev Eleazar Wheelock — Dartmouth's first president — communications were made to Huntington that gave him reason to expect election as Wheelock's successor. He actually made the journey to Hanover NH before declining. Yale conferred a D.D. honoris causa on him that same year, and he was elected a Yale Trustee 1780-1788.

narrative_blocks.header: Preached Universalism quietly for two decades
narrative_blocks.body: Old parishioners later claimed 'Dr Huntington preached good Universalism for twenty years, but in such a way that he was not suspected, except by those who relished it.' His preface to Calvinism Improved acknowledged the work 'contained a small part of a system of divinity which the author has been meditating more than twenty years' — his Coventry parish never knew their pastor's actual theology.

narrative_blocks.header: Open-air ordination at decayed Coventry meeting house
narrative_blocks.body: Installed June 29, 1763 as pastor of the First Church of Coventry, where the previous meeting house had been suffered to go to decay. The ordination services were held in the open air, whether because the building was too small or too dilapidated to be safe never made clear. Within a few years he led the parish to build the best house of public worship in the region at a cost of five thousand dollars.

death.notes: Per Huntington Family book: 'his widow [died] April 16, 1843' — five months after Amasa. Per joint memorial inscription: 'aged 69 years' (so birth corrected per Coventry CT Aug 22, 1774).

burial.plot_notes: REINTERRED December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre PA burial. Joint memorial with husband Amasa Jones. Reinterment likely facilitated by son Matthew Hale Jones (1811-1883), lawyer at Easton — deferred.

marriages.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915: m. 'Nov. 9, 1794, Amasa Jones of Coventry... son of Col. Joel Jones, of Hebron, Conn.' 9 children; 6 living as of 1858 per the book. Only TD0239 Judge Joel Jones built this stage. 8 children deferred (Talcott gen 7 cohort): Rev Joseph Huntington Jones DD 1797-1868 (Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia, Harvard 1817), Fanny Huntington 1799-1893 (Easton PA), Margaret Emeline 1801-1806 child death, Maria Allis 1803-1884 (m. William Allis Westfield NY), Eliza Wright 1805-1854 St Louis (m. Joseph J. Wright US Army surgeon), Dr Samuel H. Jones 1808-1864, Mary Joanna Harris 1809-1837 (m. Rev O. Harris), Matthew Hale Jones 1811-1883 Easton PA lawyer (→ daughter Elizabeth Huntington Jones Kirkpatrick m. US Congressman William Sebring Kirkpatrick).

narrative_blocks.header: Family moved Coventry Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Born Coventry CT August 22, 1774 to Rev Joseph Huntington and his Talcott-descendant second wife Elizabeth Hale Huntington. Married Amasa Jones of Coventry — son of Col Joel Jones of Hebron CT — November 9, 1794 and bore nine children where her father preached. In 1816 the family relocated to Wilkes-Barre PA in the Wyoming Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred Easton Pennsylvania twenty-seven years later
narrative_blocks.body: Died Wilkes-Barre PA April 16, 1843 age 68, five months after her husband Amasa. Both were originally buried Wilkes-Barre. December 10, 1870 — twenty-seven years after her death — both bodies were transferred to Easton Cemetery PA, where son Matthew Hale Jones lived as a lawyer.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-sister of Ohio Governor Samuel H. Huntington
narrative_blocks.body: Through father Rev Joseph Huntington's first marriage to Hannah Devotion, she had a half-brother Samuel H. Huntington (1765-1817) who would become the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 and chief justice of the Ohio Supreme Court. He was nine years her senior; she was the first of Joseph's nine M2 children with her Talcott-descendant mother.

death.notes: Per memorial: 'He departed this life in joyful hope of a blessed immortality through Jesus Christ November 5, A.D. 1842, Aged 72 Years.'

burial.plot_notes: Reinterred December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre burial. Joint memorial with wife TD0238.

parents.father_name: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT
parents.father_research_notes: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT — son's namesake (Joel Jr) was Judge Joel Jones TD0239 Philadelphia mayor. Not built.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre Pennsylvania in 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Born Hebron CT October 17, 1770, son of Col. Joel Jones of Hebron. Married Elizabeth Hale Huntington of Coventry CT November 9, 1794 and resided at Coventry until 1816, when the family removed to Wilkes-Barre PA — part of the post-1800 New England migration into the Wyoming and Susquehanna Valleys.

bio.title: Judge
bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT.

birth.notes: Wikipedia + biographical dictionary give Oct 26, 1795; FindAGrave gives Oct 25, 1795 — minor discrepancy. Using Wikipedia/canonical Oct 26.

marriages.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'June 14, 1831 married Eliza P. Sparhawk in Philadelphia, with whom he had six children.' Per FindAGrave 5 children named: Rev John Sparhawk Jones (TD0240 1841-1910 — built next stage); Elizabeth Jones (d.1837 young); Joel Jones (d.1837 young); Mary Jones (d.1844 young); Sarah Jones (d.1849 young). The Wikipedia '6 children' may include an unnamed sixth or a Rev John discrepancy. Only Rev John Sparhawk Jones survived to adulthood per all accounts.

education.notes: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — INST022 in tree.

education.school_name: Apprenticeship with Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT
education.notes: Studied law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT after Yale.
Litchfield Law School 1819 under Tapping Reeve — completed legal training before being admitted to the bar in New Haven.

career.notes: Conducted an academy at Wilkes-Barre PA for a year and a half starting 1822 — after family crisis prompted his relocation from New Haven CT.
Practiced law at Easton PA for many years before relocating to Philadelphia.

Secretary to Lafayette College board of trustees 1826-1835; continued as trustee through 1852. (Lafayette College not yet built as INST — pending Kirkpatrick line stage which will add 4+ connections including grandnephew William Sebring Kirkpatrick.)

1830 appointed by Governor George Wolf as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the civil code of Pennsylvania. Published 1834 'A Syllabus of the Law of Land Office Titles in Pennsylvania.' Report submitted to PA Senate January 15, 1836.

Associate Judge Philadelphia District Court 1835-1845; Presiding Judge 1845-1847. Resigned 1847 to accept Girard College presidency. Notable Delaware River fisheries ruling upholding 1783 PA-NJ compact + private fishery rights as protected property despite federal navigation/commerce supremacy.

First president of Girard College 1847-1849. Girard's 1831 will founded the college for 'poor white male orphans' with controversial clause barring clergymen from campus (upheld by SCOTUS in Vidal v. Girard's Executors 1844).

Mayor of Philadelphia October 16, 1849 to October 15, 1850. Elected as Independent (some sources Democratic) candidate October 9, 1849 narrowly defeating Whig Charles Gilpin. Lost 1850 reelection to same Gilpin. Single-year term under pre-1854 city charter.
Elected member American Philosophical Society 1848. (APS not yet built as INST.)

Ruling elder Presbyterian Church. Theological writings: Notes on Scripture (1860 posthumous, published by widow); Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age (Girard College student use); Knowledge of One Another in the Future State; edited The Literalist (5 vols 8vo prophetic literature with original additions); translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome and of the Temporal Power of the Popes' from French. Advocate of literal Second Coming prophecy.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1817 with high honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, Girard College first president

cross_connections.display_label: Federal Judge Eastern District PA; great-grandnephew via brother Matthew

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, narrow Independent victory
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Mayor of Philadelphia October 9, 1849 as the Independent (or Democratic) candidate, narrowly defeating Whig attorney Charles Gilpin to break the Whig Party's longstanding hold on the executive office. Inaugurated October 16, 1849 under the pre-1854 single-year charter. Lost his 1850 reelection bid to the same Gilpin amid the city's nativist Whig-Democrat ferment that would later fuel the Know-Nothing movement.

narrative_blocks.header: First president of Girard College, 1847-1849
narrative_blocks.body: Resigned the presiding judgeship of Philadelphia District Court 1847 to become the first president of Girard College — Stephen Girard's 1831 endowed institution for 'poor white male orphans.' Served two years amid lingering controversy from the Supreme Court's 1844 Vidal v. Girard's Executors decision upholding Girard's will clause barring clergymen from campus. Stepped down 1849 to enter the mayoral race.

narrative_blocks.header: Commissioned to revise Pennsylvania's civil code, 1830
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by Governor George Wolf in 1830 as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the Commonwealth's civil code. The team's report to the Pennsylvania Senate January 15, 1836 produced enduring statutory compilations.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1817 high honor; Litchfield Law 1819
narrative_blocks.body: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — the eldest of nine Coventry CT children. Apprenticed in law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven 1817-1819 before completing at Tapping Reeve's Litchfield Law School 1819. Admitted to the New Haven bar before family crisis prompted his relocation to Pennsylvania, where he conducted a Wilkes-Barre academy 1822-1823 and then practiced law at Easton PA for over a decade.

narrative_blocks.header: Scholar of prophecy and literal Second Coming
narrative_blocks.body: Ruling elder of the Presbyterian Church, with theological writings including the posthumous 'Notes on Scripture' (1860) and 'Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age' for Girard College students. Edited five volumes of English prophetic literature as 'The Literalist' with extensive original additions, advocating literal interpretation of scriptures predicting the Second Coming of Christ — and translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome' from the French.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT (Amasa's father). Joel Sr not built as separate entry — only known through Amasa's parentage.

burial.plot_notes: Easton Cemetery, Easton PA — family burial ground for the Jones-Kirkpatrick Easton branch.

career.notes: Practiced law at Easton PA for over four decades, residing in Easton from young adulthood until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Likely arranged 1870 reinterment of his parents
narrative_blocks.body: Almost certainly facilitated the December 10, 1870 reinterment of his parents Elizabeth and Amasa Jones from Wilkes-Barre to Easton Cemetery — twenty-seven years after his mother's death. The reinterment placed the founding generation alongside his own household in the Northampton County town where he practiced law for over forty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Anchor of the Easton Pennsylvania Jones branch
narrative_blocks.body: Followed the family migration as a child from Coventry Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre Pennsylvania in 1816 and settled in Easton as a young lawyer. His three descendants — Robert Innes, Elizabeth, and Matthew Hale Sr — produced the Kirkpatrick branch including a US Congressman, a Federal Judge, and a Paris Prize architect.

burial.plot_notes: The Woodlands Cemetery, Philadelphia — plot I-14.

education.notes: Princeton Theological Seminary — Presbyterian ministerial training.

career.notes: Pastor of Sixth Presbyterian Church Philadelphia 1838-1861 — twenty-three-year pastorate. Sixth Presbyterian was a major Old School Presbyterian congregation in Philadelphia during the antebellum era.

Author of six published theological works between 1839 and 1864: Outline of the Work of Grace (1839); Influence of Physical Causes on Religious Experience (1846); Life of Ashbel Green VDM (1849); Man Moral and Physical (1860); Autobiography of William Neill (editor, 1861); The Brazen Serpent (1864).
Secretary of the Presbyterian Relief Fund for Disabled Ministers 1861-1868, after stepping down from Sixth Presbyterian pulpit.

institutions.institution_blurb: Honorary Doctor of Divinity (DD), Lafayette College, 1843 — conferred while his brother Joel Jones (TD0239) was a Lafayette trustee.

cross_connections.display_label: Brown Memorial Baltimore founding pastor; later Calvary Philadelphia

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia, twenty-three years
narrative_blocks.body: Pastor of Sixth Presbyterian Church Philadelphia from 1838 to 1861 — a twenty-three-year tenure at a major Old School Presbyterian congregation. Stepped down 1861 to become Secretary of the Presbyterian Relief Fund for Disabled Ministers, serving in that role until his 1868 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote 1849 biography of Ashbel Green
narrative_blocks.body: His 1849 'Life of Ashbel Green VDM' became the definitive nineteenth-century treatment of Ashbel Green (1762-1848), eighth president of Princeton 1812-1822 and one of the major figures of early-American Presbyterianism. Joseph's biographical scholarship extended also to editing the 1861 Autobiography of Rev. William Neill, Dickinson College president 1824-1829.

narrative_blocks.header: Honorary Doctor of Divinity, Lafayette College, 1843
narrative_blocks.body: Received an honorary Doctor of Divinity from Lafayette College 1843 — conferred while his older brother Joel Jones was still a Lafayette trustee. Two Jones brothers thus held Lafayette honors a generation apart: Joel as founding-era board secretary and trustee, Joseph as honorary DD recipient.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — pioneer-era family cemetery; same cemetery as wife Mary Tyler Morse Huntington (I01146) and son Henry Augustus Huntington (TD0263).

career.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea.' Specific maritime career details not documented.

Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). 'A man honored by his fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence' per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record. Died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed 1819 from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Removed from Connecticut to Shelby County Indiana in 1819 — joining the first Yankee wave to the newly-admitted state (Indiana statehood 1816). His six children all born between 1811 and 1825 followed him west; he died at his Shelby County residence July 20, 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Spent some portion of his life at sea
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Huntington Family in America 1915 record: 'He had spent some portion of his life on the sea' before settling in Indiana as a respected citizen. Honored by fellow citizens with frequent testimonials of their confidence in his character.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside husband Septimus.

parents.father_research_notes: Morse family of Wrentham (Norfolk County) MA. Wrentham was the same town as the Morses (Samuel F.B. Morse the telegraph inventor's family). Specific parents not yet identified.

burial.plot_notes: Greenlawn Cemetery, Franklin, Johnson County Indiana — separate from her Huntington family at Center Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Died age thirty-five leaving four young children
narrative_blocks.body: Died June 2, 1849 at age thirty-five — leaving four surviving children Emily, Angelina, Mary Louisa, and Clarinda, per the 1888 contemporary history of her husband. Two other children (Augustus 1839-1843 and James Lawrence 1844-1844) had died young before her. Her husband Dr. James Ritchey survived her by nearly forty years and would marry twice more.

burial.plot_notes: Center Cemetery, Shelby County Indiana — alongside wife Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington and parents.

narrative_blocks.header: Three-state migration before age five, Connecticut to Indiana
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Augustus Huntington was born August 26, 1816 in Coventry Connecticut, three years before his parents' 1819 westward move. The family went first to Lebanon Ohio, then about a year later to Connersville Indiana, and in 1821 finally settled 'in the wilds of Sugar Creek township' in Shelby County Indiana, where Henry grew up. The whole multi-step journey unfolded before he reached his fifth birthday.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly one month after wife Sarah
narrative_blocks.body: Henry and his wife Sarah Ann Edwards Huntington lived on the same Sugar Creek farm from 1852 until both died in 1895. Sarah died October 26, 1895; Henry died November 26, 1895 of paralysis — exactly one month later, to the day. They had been married forty-five years.

narrative_blocks.header: Thirteen-year Sugar Creek Township trustee
narrative_blocks.body: Henry served as Sugar Creek Township trustee for thirteen years — the longest single elected term in his community's documented late-nineteenth-century record. He was a Democrat and described in his 1895 obituary simply as 'an honest' man. The trustee role oversaw township roads, poor-relief, and school funding through the years of the agrarian populist movement that reshaped Indiana politics.

bio.bio_blurb: Long-lived last survivor of her family

narrative_blocks.header: She outlived every one of her siblings
narrative_blocks.body: Of the family she was born into she lived longest by far — five of her siblings died in childhood, and she outlasted the last of the survivors by more than thirty years, reaching ninety-three.

birth.notes: WikiTree gives Feb 1, 1720-1 (Old Style/New Style transition); 1721 New Style.

death.cause: Camp fever (typhus epidemicus) contracted during Siege of Boston while serving as Captain of 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Buried near Jamaica Plain where the regiment was encamped during siege operations.

military_service.unit: 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment, Connecticut Militia (19th Regiment)
military_service.notes: Commissioned Ensign of the 4th Company in the 6th Regiment May 1764; Lieutenant October 1768; Captain of the same May 1772. Captain of the 6th Company, Col. Wolcott's Regiment in service 1776 — Siege of Boston, encamped at Jamaica Plain. Died of camp fever (typhus) Mar 7, 1776 at Roxbury Mass. DAR Ancestor #A049261. Will dated Feb 8, 1773, proved May 9, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Camp fever killed him at Siege of Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He died March 7, 1776 at Roxbury MA of camp fever — epidemic typhus — while serving as Captain of the 6th Company, Col Wolcott's CT Militia Regiment encamped at Jamaica Plain during the Siege of Boston. He was fifty-five and never saw the Declaration his fellow officers would sign four months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Will mentioned enslaved man named Newport
narrative_blocks.body: His will dated February 8, 1773 — three years before his Revolutionary death — named 'my negro man named Newport' alongside his wife and ten surviving children. The Connecticut Militia captain who died for the Revolution himself held a person enslaved, the contradiction documented in the same will that probated his patriot service.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children died in his epidemic year 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Within months of his March 7 death, his eldest son Jonathan died unmarried October 1, 1776 at age thirty, and his fifteen-year-old daughter Jerusha died September 26, 1776. The Hale household at Glastonbury lost father, son, and daughter in the same Revolutionary year.

parents.father_research_notes: Col. Thomas Welles of Glastonbury CT.
Lieut. Samuel Hale Jr. (1645-1711) of Wethersfield CT, son of Samuel Hale Sr. who came from Wales c.1637 and served in the Pequot War; SV gives 'Samuel, Jr., by his wife Mary Edwards.'
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Edwards (first wife of Samuel Hale Jr., m. 1679, d. Dec 26, 1682).

bio.title: Capt.
bio.bio_blurb: Glastonbury patriarch married to a Talcott

narrative_blocks.header: His family settled Glastonbury for generations
narrative_blocks.body: A Glastonbury man through and through, he married Sarah Talcott and raised eight children there; one son became a Revolutionary War captain. The Hales were remembered locally as people of large stature and great strength.
